  • Forward yield 3.55%
  • Payable May 23; for shareholders of record May 5; ex-div May 5.
  • See C Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Employment Situation

The most closely watched of all economic indicators, the employment situation is a set of monthly labor market indicators based on two separate reports: the establishment survey which tracks 650,000 worksites and offers the nonfarm payroll and the household survey which interviews 60,000 households and generates the unemployment rate.

Payrolls are seen up by a somewhat muted 131,000 with the jobless rate rising to 4.2 percent from 4.1 percent in February. Private payrolls are expected up 115,000.

11:25 AM Fed's Powell Speech

Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell will speak on the economic outlook before the Society for Advancing Business Editing and Writing (SABEW) Annual Conference.

12:00 PM Fed's Barr Speech

Federal Reserve Board Governor Michael Barr will speak on"Artificial Intelligence and Banking" before the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, San Francisco State University, and University of California Santa Cruz 2025 Fintech Conference.

12:45 PM Fed's Waller Speech

Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller will participate in"Payments" discussion before the 2025 New York Fed Innovation Conference.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs. Used for drilling wellbores for wells that may eventually produce oil or gas, active rigs are essential for the exploration and development of oil and gas fields.

data.title: Guess? outlines fiscal 2026 revenue growth of up to 6.2% amid strategic initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Guess?, Inc. (GES) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Carlos Alberini highlighted the completion of several strategic initiatives, including the acquisition of rag & bone, the launch of Guess Jeans targeting a younger demographic, and expanding the Guess brand in India with 22 new stores. He emphasized the importance of leveraging Guess’s operating platform across multiple brands.
  • The company reported Q4 revenues of $932 million, a 5% increase in US dollars, driven by the acquisition of rag & bone and growth in the core Guess business. Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $1.48.
  • Alberini announced key leadership appointments, including Alberto Toni as Chief Financial Officer starting in June and Lorenzo Maria Di Vecchio as General Counsel.
  • CFO Dennis Secor detailed that European wholesale grew mid-single digits despite currency headwinds, with the fall-winter collection order book up 7%. Licensing revenues grew 18%, driven by strong performance in footwear, fragrances, and handbags.

Outlook

  • Fiscal 2026 revenue growth is projected to range between 3.9% and 6.2%, supported by the full-year contribution of rag & bone and expansion in the Middle East through a joint venture with the Chalhoub Group.
  • Adjusted operating margin for the year is expected to be between 4.5% and 5.4%, with adjusted EPS guidance ranging from $1.32 to $1.76.
  • First quarter fiscal 2026 revenue growth is anticipated between 5.8% and 7.5%, with an adjusted loss per share projected between ($0.74) and ($0.65).

Financial Results

  • Q4 gross margin was reported at 44.1%, impacted by higher markdown pressure and unfavorable currency effects. Adjusted SG&A expenses rose 11% due to increased marketing investments and the addition of rag & bone.
  • For the fiscal year, revenues grew 8% in US dollars to $3 billion, with adjusted EPS reaching $1.96.
  • Inventory levels increased 21% year-over-year, primarily due to the acquisition of rag & bone and accelerated deliveries to mitigate supply chain disruptions.

Q&A

  • Mauricio Serna, UBS: Asked about growth drivers for rag & bone in FY 2026. CEO Alberini cited strong e-commerce performance, plans for new store openings, and expanding wholesale and licensing categories as key contributors.
  • Eric Beder, Small Cap Consumer Research: Inquired about the opportunity for Guess Jeans in the US and Europe. Alberini noted strong wholesale performance in Europe and plans to strengthen the US wholesale team, along with new store openings in Tokyo and West Hollywood.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about retail performance in the Americas and Asia. Queries focused on strategies to address pricing sensitivities and improve customer engagement.
  • Management expressed confidence in long-term growth opportunities, emphasizing strategic initiatives and investments in marketing. CEO Alberini stated, "We are excited about our growth opportunities... and remain fully committed to maximizing our potential."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a similar tone of cautious optimism, while analysts appeared more focused on addressing regional challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 revenue growth of 5% contrasts with 13% growth in Q3, influenced by currency headwinds and a decline in Americas retail performance.
  • Management tone remained consistent, emphasizing strategic initiatives and long-term growth, while addressing current challenges such as pricing and traffic declines.
  • Analysts continued to focus on the impact of external factors, including inflation and tariffs, on the company’s financial performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted challenges in the Americas retail segment, with a 14% decline in constant-currency comps attributed to traffic headwinds and conversion declines.
  • The Asia segment reported revenue declines, particularly in South Korea and China, due to ongoing traffic challenges.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about potential tariff impacts and the company’s ability to navigate pricing sensitivities in competitive markets.

Final Takeaway

Guess?, Inc. has outlined a strategic roadmap for fiscal 2026, targeting up to 6.2% revenue growth and emphasizing the expansion of its brand portfolio, particularly through rag & bone and Guess Jeans. While challenges persist in the Americas and Asia retail markets, management’s focus on marketing, pricing strategies, and operational efficiencies demonstrates a commitment to long-term growth and shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Simulations plus reaffirms FY 2025 guidance with 28%-33% revenue growth target
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Simulations Plus (SLP) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Shawn O’Connor highlighted a 23% year-over-year revenue increase, with 5% organic growth, driven by the Adaptive Learning & Insights (ALI) and Medical Communication (MC) business units. Adjusted EBITDA was $6.6 million, representing 29% of revenue.

  • Renewals for software products are maintaining historical levels despite funding challenges. QSP software revenue surged by 89%, primarily due to a model license for atopic dermatitis. CHEM business unit software revenue increased 8%, and there were 10 new customers alongside seven upsells.

  • Services revenue rose 34%, influenced by strong bookings in the CPP and MC units, although some project initiations have been delayed to the second half of the year. The services backlog increased to $20.4 million, up 18% sequentially.

  • CFO Will Frederick noted that software revenue comprised 60% of total revenue at $22.4 million, while services accounted for 40%. Gross margins declined to 59%, impacted by higher amortization expenses and increased service-related costs.

Outlook

  • FY 2025 guidance is reaffirmed, projecting total revenue between $90 million and $93 million, representing 28%-33% growth. Adjusted EBITDA margins are expected between 31%-33%, and adjusted diluted EPS between $1.07 and $1.20.

  • Management anticipates Q3 revenue to account for approximately 25% of FY 2025, with year-over-year growth of 21%-25%. A sequential revenue increase is forecasted for Q4.

  • O’Connor emphasized priorities including ramping up the ALI and MC business units, expanding cross-selling opportunities, and improving EBITDA margins to historical levels of 35%-40%.

Financial Results

  • Software revenue grew 16% year-over-year, with key contributions from GastroPlus (46%), MonolixSuite (23%), and ADMET Predictor (17%). ALI and MC software revenue were $0.9 million and $0.1 million, respectively.

  • Services revenue growth was led by CPP at 39% and MC at 25%. QSP and PBPK services contributed 19% and 17%, respectively. The services backlog grew to $20.4 million, reflecting an 18% increase from the prior quarter.

  • Gross margin for software was 81%, while services gross margin declined to 25%. The overall gross margin contraction was attributed to increased costs, including $0.8 million of amortization related to the Pro-ficiency acquisition.

Q&A

  • David Larsen, BTIG: Questioned software organic revenue growth and renewal rate decline. CFO Frederick clarified that organic software growth was 8%, with a renewal rate of 90%, affected by the timing of a significant account renewal.

  • Scott Schoenhaus, KeyBanc Capital Markets: Inquired about services revenue ramp and cross-selling opportunities. CEO O’Connor indicated robust pipeline activity, with a notable $5 million contract in the MC unit contributing to future revenue.

  • Christine Rains, William Blair: Asked about services backlog realization and federal funding risks. O’Connor noted that 90% of the backlog is realizable within 12 months and that federal funding reductions pose minimal risk.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on challenges in project delays and budget constraints but acknowledging strong software performance and backlog growth. Concerns about services ramp timing were prominent.

  • Management demonstrated confidence in achieving FY 2025 guidance, emphasizing resilience in their software business and strategic priorities for expansion. Prepared remarks were positive, with a slightly defensive tone during Q&A regarding delays and macroeconomic pressures.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a consistent optimism, while analysts showed increased scrutiny on services execution and backlog realization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue growth decelerated slightly from 31% in Q1 to 23% in Q2, with software organic growth also declining from 18% to 8%.

  • Services backlog increased from $17.3 million to $20.4 million, reflecting strong bookings despite delays in project starts.

  • Management reaffirmed FY 2025 guidance, maintaining confidence despite macroeconomic challenges, aligning with their prior outlook.

Risks and Concerns

  • Delays in services project initiations could potentially affect revenue timing. Management indicated that these delays are expected to push revenues to the latter half of the fiscal year.

  • Analysts raised concerns about the cautious spending environment among clients, particularly in the services segment.

  • Federal funding cuts and potential tariff impacts were noted as risks, though management minimized their direct exposure.

Final Takeaway

Simulations Plus reported strong second-quarter results, driven by robust software performance and growth in the ALI and MC business units. While services revenue faced delays, the growing backlog and reaffirmed FY 2025 guidance underscore management’s confidence. Key priorities include leveraging cross-selling opportunities and achieving historical EBITDA margin targets, with strategic focus on navigating the challenging macroeconomic environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Natgas immune to tariff turmoil so far, but LNG looks like bargaining chip in future trade deals
data.description:
U.S. natural gas bucked the sharp losses suffered by other commodities on Thursday, as the Trump administration's tariffs and their feared impact on global growth sent crude and other markets into a tailspin.

While natural gas looks somewhat insulated in the immediate term from the broader economic worries, "the larger consideration within the next 12 months is if macroeconomic disturbance... will weigh on near-term industrial and power sector demand to the extent that it derails a bullish structural setup for natural gas into winter 2025-26," according to Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics.

The biggest question, Rubin added, is how long the tariff framework stays in place, as they give President Trump "massive leverage" in trade negotiations.

"The weather is usually the driver of gas pricing and this week's cooldown that appears poised to extend into next week is currently offering support" for natgas prices, Ritterbusch analysts wrote.

The Energy Information Administration reported a 29B cf storage build for last week, a third straight increase which was within the range of expectations, and the early start to the injection season, which typically begins in April, has reduced the deficit over the year-ago and five-year average levels, Dow Jones reported.

"We expect a deficit to remain intact throughout this month and any consensus updates suggestive of a hot summer could have an exaggerated price impact," Ritterbusch said.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for May delivery closed +2% to $4.138/MMBtu, its best settlement in two weeks; U.S. natgas has gained 13.9% YTD and 133% from 52 weeks ago.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG)

Regardless of the new tariffs, the U.S. gas market looks to be affected as exports of gas in the form of LNG look set to become a bargaining chip in any ensuing trade maneuvers, according to Gavin Maguire at Reuters.

For countries seeking to trim their trade surplus with the U.S. or avoid being hit with future tariffs, commitments to scale up purchases of U.S. liquefied natural gas are an effective means of quickly rebalancing the trade ledger in favor of the U.S., but at the same time, countries affected by new tariffs that are already regular buyers of U.S. LNG may threaten to cut those purchases as part of possible reprisals, Maguire wrote.

"That means that in any event, the U.S. gas market looks set to be buffeted by the impending trade turbulence, with gas exporters, utilities, households and businesses all likely to be impacted by the upcoming swings in gas trade volumes and prices."

data.title: Crude oil loses most since July 2022 as tariffs, OPEC output hike make 'perfect bearish cocktail'
data.description:
Crude oil futures plunged by the most in nearly three years on Thursday, as concerns about weaker demand due to U.S. tariffs were exacerbated by the surprise move from OPEC+ to accelerate the return of withheld production, sending shockwaves across oil markets.

President Trump's onslaught of tariffs is ratcheting up doubts about the outlook for the global economy, and while the administration steered away from actions that would directly affect oil markets - such as measures that would have curbed flows from Canada and Mexico - concerns that the trade war will sap global energy demand hammered prices.

Then, OPEC and its allies unexpectedly said they would add 411K bbl/day of output back to the market starting next month - the equivalent of three monthly increases in a single step - citing "healthy market fundamentals" and a "positive market outlook," but a striking policy shift after years of supply constraints that had supported crude prices.

The cartel had been scheduled to raise production by 135K bbl/day in May as part of a plan to gradually unwind past cuts totaling 2.2M bbl/day.

"The perfect bearish cocktail has been mixed in Washington and in Vienna," PVM Oil Associates analyst Tamas Varga said in response to the twin moves. "The reciprocal tariffs on virtually every salient U.S. trading partner justifiably raise the fears of recession and possibly stagflation. Economic and oil demand growth is adversely impacted."

The OPEC+ decision to bring back more production than expected adds downside risk to crude prices, while the negative effect on global growth from U.S. tariffs could curb oil demand growth by 250K-500K bbl/day, “close to half of our 2025 growth forecast of 1.1M bbl/day at the upper end," UBS analysts wrote. "We see the combination of this leading to increased downside risk for crude oil prices towards the lower end of the $55-$75/bbl WTI range."

Citi Research offered a more upbeat view, maintaining its Q2 Brent price forecast of $68/bbl, saying "These withering trade tariff developments arrive at a time when the macroeconomic momentum was already softening... Yet sanctions on Iranian, Venezuelan, and eventually Russian oil purchases act to tighten supply, thereby offsetting impacts from goods tariffs."

Further weighing on market sentiment, data from the U.S. Energy Information Administration showed U.S. crude inventories rose by a surprisingly large 6.2M barrels last week, compared to expectations for a substantial decline.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery finished -6.6% to $66.95/bbl, and front-month June Brent crude (CO1:COM) ended -6.4% to $70.14/bbl, the lowest settlement value for both benchmarks since mid-March and the sharpest one-day percentage decline for both since July 12, 2022.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (NYSEARCA:XLE), (XOP)

Energy company shares took a beating, with U.S. producers, pipeline companies and oilfield services firms all down sharply, including the top 10 energy names ranked by market value: Exxon Mobil (XOM) -5.2%, Chevron (CVX) -6.2%, ConocoPhillips (COP) -10.2%, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) -1.7%, Williams Companies (WMB) -4.2%, EOG Resources (EOG) -7.7%, Kinder Morgan (KMI) -4.1%, Energy Transfer (ET) -6.3%, SLB (SLB) -7%, Oneok (OKE) -7.6%.

Also among the day's biggest losers: Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN) -19%, Liberty Energy (LBRT) -18.4%, APA Corp. (APA) -16.5%, Helmerich & Payne (HP) -15.2%, Valero Energy (VLO) -14.4%, Nabors Industries (NBR) -14.4%, Valaris (VAL) -13.9%, Transocean (RIG) -13.9%, Phillips 66 (PSX) -13.6%, Marathon Petroleum (MPC) -13%, Halliburton (HAL) -12.8%, Noble Corp. (NE) -12.8%, Diamondback Energy (FANG) -12.5%, Devon Energy (DVN) -12.5%, Texas Pacific Land (TPL) -12.2%, Ovintiv (OVV) -11.5%, Occidental Petroleum (OXY) -10.9%, Hess (HES) -7.2%.

data.title: Lifecore Biomedical targets 12% revenue CAGR and expanded CDMO pipeline by 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lifecore Biomedical (LFCR) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Paul Josephs highlighted key achievements, including signing multiple agreements with new and existing customers, maintaining strong revenues aligned with guidance, and strengthening the cash balance through the sale of unused assets. Operational improvements were implemented to enhance margins and efficiencies.
  • CFO Ryan Lake stated that revenues for Q3 were $35.2 million, reflecting a slight decrease from the prior year due to timing and lifecycle completions of customer projects. Gross profit was $9.8 million, and adjusted EBITDA was $5.7 million. He emphasized the company's steady financial performance and reiterated fiscal year guidance.
  • Josephs noted progress on strategic goals, including a partnership expansion with a large multinational company expected to significantly increase volume demand by 2027. Additionally, milestones were achieved in advancing late-stage pipeline programs toward commercialization, with 10 programs poised for potential approval by 2028.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed fiscal 2025 revenue guidance of $126.5 million to $130 million and adjusted EBITDA guidance of $19 million to $21 million. They expressed confidence in achieving cash flow breakeven or slight positivity in the second half of the year.
  • Strategic goals include driving a 12% revenue CAGR and increasing EBITDA margins to over 25% in the coming years.
  • A new customer agreement was signed with Humanetics, focusing on their BIO 300 program, which reflects Lifecore’s expansion into diverse modalities.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter was $35.2 million, down 2% year-over-year due to decreases in CDMO revenues, offset by growth in hyaluronic acid (HA) manufacturing revenues.
  • Gross profit declined to $9.8 million, impacted by inventory adjustments and timing of customer projects. Selling, general, and administrative expenses were $10.1 million, slightly up due to litigation-related costs.
  • The company reported a net loss of $14.8 million, compared to net income of $15.6 million in the prior year, which had included a one-time debt derivative adjustment.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $5.7 million, reflecting a slight year-over-year decrease primarily due to gross profit decline.

Q&A

  • Matt Hewitt, Craig-Hallum Capital Group, asked about domestic drug manufacturing trends. CEO Josephs noted increasing discussions on Western manufacturing and its potential future impact on Lifecore’s business.
  • Hewitt inquired about the Humanetics contract. Josephs explained it involves a Phase 2 program transferred from another manufacturer.
  • Michael Petusky, Barrington Research, asked about cash flow and CapEx. CFO Lake reported positive cash flow from operations of $2 million and highlighted expectations of cash flow neutrality or slight positivity for the second half of the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed interest in Lifecore's strategic goals and pipeline developments but raised concerns regarding financial losses and litigation costs.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing progress on strategic objectives and operational improvements. Josephs expressed optimism regarding future opportunities and pipeline expansion.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management tone remained consistent, highlighting progress on initiatives and steady execution of the growth strategy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance remained consistent, with revenue and EBITDA targets reiterated. Strategic focus on late-stage program commercialization and CDMO pipeline expansion continued.
  • Analysts maintained focus on financial discipline, cash flow, and operational expense reduction.
  • Management confidence in achieving strategic goals and financial targets remained strong.
  • SG&A expenses saw a sequential reduction, reflecting ongoing cost management efforts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Litigation expenses related to legacy legal matters and class action lawsuits increased costs.
  • Dependence on timing of customer projects and lifecycle completions impacted revenue and profit.
  • Management is addressing these challenges through cost reductions, operational improvements, and expanded customer engagements.

Final Takeaway

Lifecore Biomedical is advancing its growth strategy through new customer acquisitions, expanded CDMO capabilities, and late-stage program progress. Operational improvements and fiscal discipline are contributing to better margins and cash flow positioning. With a focus on achieving a 12% revenue CAGR and enhanced EBITDA margins by 2027, the company is building a pipeline poised for mid-term commercialization success.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Musk's X could be fined $1B+ for breaching EU's DSA law - NYT
data.description:
European watchdogs are reportedly looking to fine billionaire Elon Musk's social media platform X more than $1B for not complying with the bloc's Digital Services Act, which is likely to amplify tensions between the region and the U.S.

According to a New York Times report from Thursday, citing four sources, EU regulators are "preparing major penalties" against X for breaking the DSA law aimed at combatting illicit content and disinformation.

The report said the fines and demands for product changes are expected to be announced this summer, which would be the first issued under the new EU law intended to force social media companies to police their own services.

The fine is expected to come amid an ongoing back-and-forth trade dispute, reciprocal tariffs, and the Ukraine war.

One of the sources told NYT that the fine could surpass $1B, as the EU seeks to "make an example of X to deter other companies from violating the law."

The EU probe against X began in 2023, and the regulator preliminarily found last year that Musk's platform had violated the DSA law. The parties could, however, reach a settlement if the company agrees to changes to satisfy regulators.

X is also the subject of a second EU probe that is "broader and that could lead to further fines," the report said.

For the second probe, the EU is trying to show that X’s hands-off approach for user-generated content has caused hate speech, disinformation, and material that is undercutting the bloc's democracy to rise on the platform, two sources told NYT.

"We have always enforced and will continue to enforce our laws fairly and without discrimination toward all companies operating in the EU, in full compliance with global rules," a European Commission spokesman told the NYT.

The EU and X have been in discussions for months over the investigation. After the preliminary judgment against X last year, the company replied with hundreds of points of dispute that regulators have been working through to rebut, two officials told NYT.

Under the DSA, companies can be fined up to 6% of global revenue. The exact penalty against X would not be decided until closer to a final announcement.

More on X

  • Elon Musk says xAI has acquired X in all-stock deal
  • X adds more companies to lawsuit over advertising boycott: report
  • Banks prepare to sell billions of dollars of X loans: report
  • Elon Musk's X partners with Visa in financial services push

data.title: Soybeans seen as most affected among U.S. grains from Trump's latest tariffs
data.description:
Soybeans likely will feel the strongest fallout from tariffs, traders determined Thursday after spending the session processing what President Trump's new "reciprocal" tariffs will mean for U.S. grains, sending soybean futures sharply lower while wheat and corn finished nearly flat.

"China is a major importer of U.S. soybeans and while their buying tends to shift to Brazil at this time of year, demand going forward could be heavily impacted," Tomm Pfitzenmaier of Summit Commodity Brokerage wrote.

The additional 34% tariff levied on Chinese goods is expected to cut into demand for U.S., but the timing of the announcement means that the impact may not be immediate, according to analysts.

"China is out of the market for U.S. beans right now anyway," StoneX analyst said, noting that China at this time of year is more interested in procuring Brazilian soybeans, as Brazil's harvest of its soybean crop nears its end.

Some traders said the fact that Canada and Mexico avoided fresh tariffs could be a sign of improving negotiations between the U.S. and its neighbors.

On the CBOT, soybeans (S_1:COM) for May delivery settled -1.7% to $10.12 1/2 per bushel, while wheat (W_1:COM) for May delivery closed -0.4% to $5.37 per bushel and corn (C_1:COM) for May delivery finished +0.2% at $4.58 1/2 per bushel.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:SOYB), (NYSEARCA:WEAT), (NYSEARCA:CORN), (DBA), (MOO)

The American Farm Bureau Federation, the leading farm lobby, said the tariffs threaten U.S. farmers' competitiveness and could cause long-term damage by eroding market share.

"We share the administration's goal of leveling the playing field with our international partners, but increased tariffs threaten the economic sustainability of farmers who have lost money on most major crops for the past three years," group president Zippy Duvall said.

data.title: AbbVie cuts Q1, fiscal 2025 EPS forecast on IPR&D, milestones expense
data.description:
• AbbVie said it is lowering its Q1 and fiscal 2025 EPS guidance due to acquired IPR&D and milestones expense.
  • Those expenses will result in a $248M impact on a pre-tax basis, representing a negative impact of $0.13 to both GAAP diluted earnings per share and adjusted non-GAAP diluted earnings per share.  
  • Q1 EPS guidance is now seen at $2.34-2.38, (from $2.47-2.51 prior) excluding non-recurring items. Consensus is $2.51.
  • FY 2025 EPS is now projected at $11.99-12.19, (from $12.12-12.32) excluding non-recurring items. Consensus is $12.28.
